---
title: "Building geokrige: A Python Package for Spatial Interpolation"
author: "Sheldon Downs"
date: "2026-07-10"
categories: [code, tools, spatial statistics]
image: "geokrige_hex_sticker (1).png"
---

## Why I Built It

While working on my NYC traffic spatial interpolation project I wanted a way to 
streamline the full Kriging pipeline into something clean and reusable. Rather 
than having every step scattered across a notebook, I wanted a single workflow 
that could be applied to any city or any type of spatial point data with minimal 
changes.

So I built **geokrige**. It was developed using NYC traffic data but is designed 
to work with any dataset where you have a location and a numeric value — air 
quality monitors, weather stations, real estate prices, soil samples, and more.

## What It Does

`fetch → aggregate → Moran's I → variogram fit → Kriging → cross-validate → export`

| Module | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `socrata.py` | Pull data from any Socrata open data API |
| `data.py` | Load, aggregate, build sensor pairs, test stationarity |
| `autocorrelation.py` | Compute Moran's I |
| `variogram.py` | Fit empirical variogram — spherical, exponential, or gaussian |
| `kriging.py` | Auto-select Ordinary vs Universal Kriging, predict, cross-validate |
| `export.py` | Export to Folium heatmap, GeoTIFF, or shapefile |

## Key Design Decisions

The spatial heavy lifting is pushed into **SedonaDB** rather than GeoPandas, 
making the pipeline scale to large datasets without a full Spark cluster.

Ordinary vs Universal Kriging is selected automatically based on a stationarity 
test — no manual decision required.

## Try It

If your city publishes point sensor data through a Socrata open data portal, 
you can run the full pipeline with minimal setup.

Full source code and documentation:

[github.com/SheldonDowns/geokrige](https://github.com/SheldonDowns/geokrige)
